q: Who is accountable for maximizing the value of the product resulting from the work of the Scrum Team?
options: The Scrum Master
The Product Owner
The Developers
The stakeholders
why: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product. This is the central accountability of the role per the Scrum Guide 2020.

q: The Product Owner is:
options: A committee of business representatives
One person, not a committee
A role shared between a business owner and a tech lead
Any Developer who knows the business
why: The Product Owner is one person, not a committee. They may represent the needs of many stakeholders, but the accountability cannot be divided.

q: The Product Owner may delegate work related to the Product Backlog to others, but remains accountable.
options: True
False
why: True. The Product Owner may do the work themselves or delegate it, but they remain accountable for the outcome.

q: Who has final authority over the order of the Product Backlog?
options: The Scrum Master
The most senior stakeholder
The Product Owner
The Developers by consensus
why: The Product Owner is accountable for ordering the Product Backlog. Others may influence or suggest order, but the decision rests with the Product Owner.

q: Who has the authority to cancel a Sprint?
options: The Scrum Master
The Developers
Only the Product Owner
Stakeholders or management
why: Only the Product Owner can cancel a Sprint. This would typically happen if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete.

q: Which of the following are characteristics of an effective Product Owner? (Select all that apply)
options: Has authority over the Product Backlog and ordering decisions
Is available to the Scrum Team and stakeholders
Has deep knowledge of the product domain and customers
Personally writes every Product Backlog item
Makes technical implementation decisions for the Developers
Orders the Product Backlog to maximize value
why: An effective Product Owner has authority, is available, is domain-knowledgeable, and orders the backlog for maximum value. Writing every item personally isn't required — anyone can contribute items. Technical implementation decisions belong to the Developers.

q: For a Product Owner to succeed, the entire organization must respect their decisions.
options: True
False
why: True. The Scrum Guide 2020 explicitly states that for Product Owners to succeed, the entire organization must respect their decisions.

q: The Product Owner must personally write every Product Backlog item.
options: True
False
why: False. Anyone on the Scrum Team, or even stakeholders, can draft Product Backlog items. The Product Owner is accountable for the backlog as a whole, including what gets added, ordered, and refined.

q: The Product Backlog is:
options: A fixed scope document signed off at the start of the project
An emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product
A list of technical tasks for the Developers
A prioritized feature roadmap owned by stakeholders
why: The Product Backlog is an emergent and ordered list of what is needed to improve the product. It is the single source of work undertaken by the Scrum Team.

q: Product Backlog refinement is best described as:
options: A formal Scrum event held weekly
A time-boxed meeting led by the Scrum Master
An ongoing activity to add detail, estimates, and order to items
An activity the Product Owner does alone
why: Refinement is an ongoing activity — not a formal event. The Scrum Team collaboratively adds detail, estimates, and order to items. The Product Owner is accountable for the outcome.

q: A Product Backlog item is considered 'Done' when:
options: The Product Owner accepts it
It is deployed to production
It meets the Definition of Done
All acceptance criteria are ticked
why: An item is Done when it meets the Definition of Done. At that moment, it becomes part of the Increment.

q: Which attributes are commonly associated with Product Backlog items? (Select all that apply)
options: Description
Order
Size (estimate)
Value
Name of the developer assigned to it
A firm delivery date
why: Scrum Guide attributes of Product Backlog items are typically description, order, size, and value. Scrum does not assign items to specific developers, and does not require firm delivery dates on items.

q: What should guide the Product Owner in ordering the Product Backlog?
options: Strictly the estimated effort, lowest first
Stakeholder seniority and political power
Value, risk, priority, dependencies, and what best achieves product goals
Development team velocity
why: The Product Owner orders the Product Backlog based on what best achieves the product's goals — weighing value, risk, priority, and dependencies. It is not a pure effort-sort or politics-sort.

q: How much of the Product Backlog should be refined at any given time?
options: The entire Product Backlog at all times
Enough that items for the next Sprint are 'ready' and understood
Only the top item
Exactly 10% of total effort per the Scrum Guide
why: Refinement is ongoing. Items near the top of the Product Backlog should be refined enough to be ready for Sprint Planning. The '10%' rule is not in the 2020 Scrum Guide.

q: Anyone can suggest items for the Product Backlog, but the Product Owner decides what is added and how it is ordered.
options: True
False
why: True. Anyone can propose items. The Product Owner is accountable for what ends up in the Product Backlog and in what order.

q: Transparency of the Product Backlog is essential for empirical product management.
options: True
False
why: True. Transparency is one of the three pillars of empiricism. Without a transparent Product Backlog, inspection and adaptation are compromised.

q: The Product Goal describes:
options: A short-term delivery target for the next Sprint
A future state of the product — a long-term objective for the Scrum Team
A binding release commitment to customers
A list of features to be built this quarter
why: The Product Goal is the long-term objective for the Scrum Team — a future state of the product. It provides direction for the Scrum Team's work.

q: The Product Goal is the commitment for which artifact?
options: The Sprint Backlog
The Increment
The Product Backlog
The Definition of Done
why: The three commitments: Product Goal commits to the Product Backlog, Sprint Goal commits to the Sprint Backlog, Definition of Done commits to the Increment.

q: How many Product Goals can a Scrum Team pursue at the same time?
options: As many as stakeholders request
One
One per Sprint
One per release
why: A Scrum Team focuses on one Product Goal at a time. They must fulfill (or abandon) the current Product Goal before taking on the next.

q: Who is accountable for developing and explicitly communicating the Product Goal?
options: The Scrum Master
The Developers
The Product Owner
The stakeholders
why: The Product Owner is accountable for developing and explicitly communicating the Product Goal — a core responsibility listed in the Scrum Guide 2020.

q: The Product Goal is contained in the Product Backlog.
options: True
False
why: True. The Scrum Guide 2020 states: 'The Product Goal is in the Product Backlog. The rest of the Product Backlog emerges to define what will fulfill the Product Goal.'

q: What is the primary purpose of the Sprint Review?
options: A formal acceptance meeting where stakeholders sign off
A status report to management
Inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog through collaboration
Retrospect on the Sprint process
why: The Sprint Review is a working session to inspect the Increment and discuss what to do next. The Product Backlog may be adjusted based on new information. It is not a sign-off meeting or a status report.

q: During Sprint Planning, who proposes how the product could increase its value in the current Sprint?
options: The Scrum Master
The Product Owner
The Developers
Stakeholders
why: In Sprint Planning Topic One ('Why is this Sprint valuable?'), the Product Owner proposes how the product could increase its value. The whole Scrum Team then collaborates to define a Sprint Goal.

q: Who must attend the Daily Scrum?
options: The entire Scrum Team plus stakeholders
The Developers. The Product Owner attends if actively working on items
The Product Owner and Scrum Master only
The Scrum Master leads it
why: The Daily Scrum is for the Developers. The Product Owner or Scrum Master participate only if actively working on Sprint Backlog items as Developers.

q: The Product Owner should attend the Sprint Retrospective.
options: True
False
why: True. The Sprint Retrospective is for the whole Scrum Team, including the Product Owner. Its purpose is to identify improvements to people, interactions, processes, tools, and the Definition of Done.

q: Who determines how many Product Backlog items are selected for a Sprint?
options: The Product Owner, based on business priorities
The Scrum Master, based on past velocity
The Developers, forecasting what they can accomplish — in collaboration with the Product Owner
Stakeholders, during Sprint Planning
why: The Developers forecast what they can accomplish during the Sprint. They collaborate with the Product Owner on selection, but the capacity decision belongs to the Developers.

q: An Increment must be released to customers by the end of the Sprint.
options: True
False
why: False. The Increment must meet the Definition of Done by the end of the Sprint, but release to customers is a separate decision made by the Product Owner.

q: How often can Increments potentially be delivered to users?
options: Only at the end of each Sprint
Once per release cycle
Multiple times per Sprint — any time an item meets the Definition of Done
Only after stakeholder approval at Sprint Review
why: Multiple Increments may be created within a single Sprint. An Increment can be delivered to stakeholders as soon as it meets the Definition of Done — no need to wait for Sprint end or Review.

q: The Product Owner decides when to release an Increment to customers.
options: True
False
why: True. The release decision rests with the Product Owner, who balances value, risk, and market considerations.

q: A stakeholder approaches the Developers mid-Sprint with an urgent request. What is the most appropriate response?
options: The Developers add it immediately to be responsive
The Scrum Master adds it to the Sprint Backlog
The request is directed to the Product Owner, who may adjust the backlog or renegotiate Sprint scope
The Sprint is cancelled to allow replanning
why: Stakeholder requests go through the Product Owner. The PO decides whether the request enters the Product Backlog, and may renegotiate Sprint scope with the Developers — as long as the Sprint Goal is not compromised.

q: Two senior stakeholders disagree strongly on what should be prioritized next. What should the Product Owner do?
options: Escalate the decision to executive leadership
Let the stakeholders vote
Use their own judgment to order by value and communicate the rationale
Split the Sprint 50/50 between both requests
why: The Product Owner is the single accountable person for maximizing product value. They take input from stakeholders but make the ordering decision themselves, with clear rationale.

q: The Product Owner should measure product success primarily through:
options: Developer velocity and story points completed
Number of features delivered per Sprint
Outcomes and value delivered to customers and business
On-time, on-budget delivery against the plan
why: Scrum is empirical and value-driven. A Product Owner focuses on outcomes — did this increase customer or business value? — rather than output metrics like velocity or feature count.

q: The Sprint Review is the only appropriate time for the Product Owner to engage stakeholders.
options: True
False
why: False. The Sprint Review is one structured opportunity for stakeholder engagement, but the Product Owner may and should collaborate with stakeholders throughout the Sprint as needed.

q: When multiple Scrum Teams work together on the same product, they share:
options: One Scrum Master and one Sprint Backlog
One Product Goal, one Product Backlog, and one Product Owner
The same Sprint Goal across all teams
Different Product Owners per component
why: Even at scale, a single product has one Product Goal, one Product Backlog, and one Product Owner. This preserves a single source of direction and value.

q: Which are the three pillars of empiricism? (Select all that apply)
options: Transparency
Inspection
Adaptation
Commitment
Focus
why: Empiricism in Scrum rests on three pillars: Transparency, Inspection, and Adaptation. Commitment and Focus are Scrum Values, not pillars.

q: Which are the five Scrum Values? (Select all that apply)
options: Commitment
Focus
Openness
Respect
Courage
Trust
Transparency
why: The five Scrum Values are Commitment, Focus, Openness, Respect, and Courage. Trust is a consequence of living the values. Transparency is a pillar of empiricism, not a value.

q: The Sprint Goal is:
options: A list of all work selected for the Sprint
The single objective for the Sprint and the commitment for the Sprint Backlog
A target release date
The Product Owner's forecast for the quarter
why: The Sprint Goal is the single objective for the Sprint. It is the commitment for the Sprint Backlog and is created collaboratively by the Scrum Team during Sprint Planning.

q: The Product Owner is asked to push a release date forward despite the Developers warning that quality would suffer. The Product Owner should:
options: Override the Developers — release dates are the PO's authority
Collaborate with the Developers to understand trade-offs, potentially reducing scope rather than quality
Cancel the Sprint and replan from scratch
Escalate to management and let them decide
why: The Product Owner maximizes product value — long-term as well as short-term. Shipping below the Definition of Done creates technical debt and erodes value. The healthy path is scope negotiation with the Developers, not quality compromise.

q: Ordering the Product Backlog means:
options: Sorting items alphabetically
Arranging items by what delivers the most value in that order
Letting the highest-paid stakeholder decide
Using only story points
why: Ordering is about value. Many inputs feed the decision — value, risk, dependencies, learning — but the PO is accountable for the final order.

q: A Product Owner spends most Sprints writing detailed specifications. Developers rarely speak to stakeholders directly. What is the likely problem?
options: The Scrum Master should write the specifications instead
The Product Owner has slipped into being a requirements analyst rather than a value-maximizer
Nothing — this is how the Product Owner accountability is meant to work
The Developers should take over stakeholder communication
why: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing value, not for producing detailed specifications. When the PO becomes a spec-writing middle layer and Developers are disconnected from stakeholders, the team loses the direct collaboration empirical product management depends on.

q: For the Product Owner to succeed, the entire organization must:
options: Micromanage the PO's decisions
Respect the Product Owner's decisions
Let Developers override the PO
Delegate all PO responsibilities to a committee
why: Per the Scrum Guide 2020: 'For Product Owners to succeed, the entire organization must respect their decisions.' These decisions are visible in the Product Backlog and Increment.

q: Stakeholder pressure is pushing the Product Owner to include low-value items in the next Sprint. The Product Owner should:
options: Comply — stakeholders fund the product
Accept input but decide based on value, communicating rationale clearly
Defer the decision to the Scrum Master
Split the Sprint across unrelated work
why: The PO is accountable for maximizing value. Stakeholders are input sources; ordering is the PO's decision. Transparency about rationale builds trust.

q: Which Product Backlog item attributes are commonly used?
options: Description, order, size, and sometimes value estimate
Only story points
Assignee, due date, and priority label
Author, approver, and department
why: PBIs often have: description, order, size, and may include value. The PO is accountable for these. Size is the Developers' call; value is the PO's.

q: The Product Backlog is ever-changing as long as the product exists.
options: True
False
why: True. The Product Backlog is dynamic and emerges as the product and its environment evolve. Refinement is an ongoing activity.

q: A Product Backlog item is 'ready' for selection in Sprint Planning when:
options: The PO has signed off on acceptance criteria
It is small enough to fit in a Sprint and sufficiently understood by the Developers
All edge cases are exhaustively documented
QA has reviewed it
why: Scrum doesn't formalize 'ready', but PBIs selected into a Sprint should be sized and understood well enough that Developers can reasonably commit to delivering them.

q: Product Backlog refinement typically involves:
options: The Product Owner alone
The Scrum Team collaborating to break down items and size them
The Scrum Master assigning tasks
Stakeholders voting on items
why: Refinement is a Scrum Team activity — the PO drives value and order, the Developers contribute sizing and technical insight, and the SM facilitates.

q: Estimating Product Backlog items is the Product Owner's accountability.
options: True
False
why: False. The people performing the work — the Developers — are responsible for sizing. The PO may influence by helping them understand trade-offs.

q: Who performs the estimation of Product Backlog items?
options: The Product Owner
The Scrum Master
The Developers
Stakeholders
why: Developers are accountable for sizing since they perform the work. They may seek input from the PO to understand business context.

q: When is a Product Goal abandoned?
options: Never — it is fixed
When obsolete, a new Product Goal replaces it
Only at the end of the fiscal quarter
When stakeholders vote to change it
why: A Scrum Team pursues one Product Goal at a time. It may be abandoned if it becomes obsolete; a new Product Goal then replaces it. The PO makes this call.

q: How does the Product Goal relate to the Product Backlog?
options: They are unrelated
The Product Goal is the commitment for the backlog; the Backlog emerges to fulfill the Goal
The Product Backlog replaces the Product Goal
The Product Goal is ordered within the Sprint Backlog
why: The Product Goal is the long-term objective and commitment for the Product Backlog. The Product Backlog emerges to define what will fulfill the Product Goal.

q: A Scrum Team can pursue multiple Product Goals simultaneously.
options: True
False
why: False. A Scrum Team pursues one Product Goal at a time. The current Goal must be fulfilled or abandoned before a new one is adopted.

q: Evidence-Based Management (EBM) helps organizations:
options: Enforce uniform processes across teams
Measure and improve value delivery through empirical evidence and experimentation
Replace Scrum Masters with dashboards
Track developer productivity per hour
why: EBM is a framework for improving value delivery through evidence — outcomes, goals, and measured experiments — rather than output or activity.

q: Which of the following are Key Value Areas (KVAs) in Evidence-Based Management? (Select all that apply)
options: Current Value
Unrealized Value
Velocity
Ability to Innovate
Time to Market
Cost Efficiency
why: EBM defines four Key Value Areas: Current Value (CV), Unrealized Value (UV), Ability to Innovate (A2I), and Time to Market (T2M). Velocity and cost efficiency are not KVAs.

q: Which of these is an outcome-based measure?
options: Number of features released
Story points completed per Sprint
Customer satisfaction or retention
Lines of code produced
why: Outcome measures reflect value delivered (satisfaction, retention, revenue, usage). Output measures (features, story points, LoC) are activity, not value.

q: Which approach best reflects empirical product management?
options: Build the complete product to the initial plan
Release, measure, learn, adapt — iteratively based on real-world feedback
Defer all releases until the product is feature-complete
Spend a year on detailed requirements before building
why: Empirical product management uses frequent releases and validated learning to decide what to build next. It embraces uncertainty rather than pretending plans are reliable.

q: Velocity is a reliable measure of the value a product delivers.
options: True
False
why: False. Velocity measures throughput, not value. A team can move fast and still build the wrong things. Value requires outcome measurement.

q: Which Evidence-Based Management measure best captures the value the product delivers to users today?
options: Velocity of the Scrum Team
Number of Product Backlog items completed per Sprint
Current Value — the value the product delivers to users today
Number of releases shipped this quarter
why: Current Value is the EBM Key Value Area that captures the value the product actually delivers to users right now. Velocity and item counts describe activity; release count describes throughput. Value is measured by outcomes for customers and the business.

q: If an Increment meets the Definition of Done mid-Sprint, the Product Owner should:
options: Wait for the Sprint Review to release it
Decide whether releasing immediately creates more value than waiting
Cancel the Sprint
Get stakeholder approval first
why: An Increment can be released any time it meets the DoD. The PO balances value, risk, and timing to make release decisions.

q: A Minimum Viable Product (MVP) is:
options: A buggy prototype released to meet deadlines
The smallest release that delivers enough value to validate learning from real users
The final polished version of the product
A demo shown only to internal stakeholders
why: MVP is a learning tool — the smallest release that enables validated learning from real users. It tests hypotheses about value, not corner-cuts quality.

q: When forecasting future delivery, the Product Owner should:
options: Provide firm commitments with fixed scope and dates
Provide forecasts based on empirical data, acknowledging they change as learning occurs
Refuse to forecast at all
Delegate forecasting to the Scrum Master
why: Empirical forecasting draws on actual throughput and outcomes. Forecasts are honest probabilistic statements, not binding long-range commitments.

q: How often should the Product Owner engage stakeholders?
options: Only at the Sprint Review
Continuously throughout the Sprint when collaboration helps
Once per release cycle
Only through formal reports
why: The Sprint Review is one structured checkpoint, but the PO engages stakeholders continuously. Frequent interaction protects against building the wrong thing.

q: The top of the Product Backlog is full of large, vague items that Developers cannot plan against. What should happen?
options: The Product Owner decides alone which items are ready
The Scrum Team collaboratively refines items so top-of-backlog items are small and clear
Developers refuse to plan until the Product Owner has written full specifications
Sprint Planning is skipped until the entire backlog is refined
why: Product Backlog refinement is an ongoing collaborative activity. The Product Owner is accountable for the backlog, but refining items — breaking them down, adding detail, ordering — works best as a shared activity so Developers can surface questions and reach shared understanding.

q: The best indicator that a product is delivering value is:
options: Continuous high velocity over multiple Sprints
Consistently low defect counts in releases
Measurable positive outcomes for customers and business (usage, satisfaction, revenue, retention)
Delivering more features per Sprint than competitors
why: Value is measured through outcomes, not output. Velocity, feature count, and bug counts can be misleading proxies.

q: 'Time to Market' in Evidence-Based Management refers to:
options: The length of Sprint Planning
The organization's ability to deliver new capabilities and services quickly
The time from hiring to onboarding
The elapsed time between Sprint events
why: Time to Market (T2M) measures an organization's capacity to deliver new value to customers quickly. Shorter T2M typically increases the rate of learning and value capture.

q: A Product Owner is unsure whether a proposed feature will deliver value. The best approach is to:
options: Avoid building it
Build the complete feature to be safe
Build a small, testable version and measure the outcome
Delegate the decision to stakeholders
why: When uncertainty is high, small experiments and validated learning outperform big upfront bets. This is empirical product management in action.

q: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing value — including long-term value, not only short-term delivery.
options: True
False
why: True. The PO's accountability for value spans time horizons. Short-term wins that damage long-term value are a bad trade.

q: A product vision:
options: Is a detailed list of every feature
Describes a future state and direction, aligning the Scrum Team and stakeholders
Is the same as the Product Goal
Is strictly mandated by the Scrum Guide
why: A product vision sets overarching direction. The Scrum Guide doesn't mandate it, but in practice it anchors the Product Goal and the PO's ordering decisions.

q: During Sprint Planning, who is accountable for ensuring a Sprint Goal is defined?
options: The Product Owner alone
The Developers alone
The entire Scrum Team collaborates on the Sprint Goal
The Scrum Master writes it
why: The Sprint Goal is crafted collaboratively. The PO proposes value; the whole Scrum Team shapes the Goal. The Sprint Goal is the commitment for the Sprint Backlog.

q: A Scrum Team has been pursuing a Product Goal for several months, and recent market evidence suggests the Goal no longer reflects what customers need. The Product Owner should:
options: Keep working toward the original Goal to honor the commitment
Inspect whether the Goal is still valid and, if not, replace it with one that reflects current learning
Cancel the product and start a new one from scratch
Ask the Developers to work harder during the next few Sprints
why: A Product Goal is a long-term commitment, but not an immovable one. When evidence shows the Goal is no longer valid, the Product Owner inspects and adapts — replacing it with a Goal that reflects what has been learned. Clinging to an invalid Goal violates empiricism.

q: Which of the following happen during a Sprint Review? (Select all that apply)
options: The Scrum Team and stakeholders inspect the Increment
The Product Backlog may be adjusted based on new information
Stakeholders formally sign off on completed work
The Scrum Team gets approval to release
Progress toward the Product Goal is discussed
why: Sprint Review is a working session: inspect the Increment, adapt the Product Backlog, discuss progress toward the Product Goal. It is not a sign-off or approval meeting.

q: How does the Product Owner collaborate with Developers during the Sprint?
options: Not at all — Developers work independently
Continuously — answering questions and making trade-off decisions
Only during Sprint Planning
Through written tickets only
why: Ongoing collaboration is essential. Decisions about scope, clarity of items, and value trade-offs happen throughout the Sprint, not only at events.

q: When scaling Scrum to multiple teams on the same product:
options: Each team has its own Product Owner
There is still one Product Owner accountable for the product
Teams work on separate products
No Product Owner is needed
why: Even at scale, a single product has one Product Goal, one Product Backlog, and one Product Owner — preserving a single source of value direction.

q: How does empiricism apply to product management?
options: By avoiding experimentation
By using observation and experimentation to discover what delivers value
By strictly adhering to a long-term plan
By requiring signoff before every decision
why: Empiricism says knowledge comes from experience and decisions are based on what's observed. In product management, that means measuring, learning, and adapting.

q: How should the Product Owner align product decisions with business strategy?
options: They shouldn't — Scrum ignores business strategy
Ensure the Product Goal and ordering reflect and advance strategic objectives
Defer all decisions to executives
Maximize feature count
why: The Product Goal translates strategy into a product direction. Ordering reflects what best advances that direction, given current evidence.

q: Which of the following is MOST important for the Product Owner to focus on?
options: Writing detailed specifications
Estimating work accurately
Maximizing product value through Product Goal and ordering
Managing Developer assignments
why: The PO's core accountability is value maximization. Specifications and estimates serve that end but aren't the PO's core job.

q: Product Backlog items near the top should be more granular and better understood than items further down.
options: True
False
why: True. Refinement progressively decomposes and clarifies items as they move up in order. Items far down can be coarse; items near the top must be actionable soon.

q: A Product Owner realizes a Product Backlog item is no longer needed. They should:
options: Complete it to avoid wasted work
Remove it or deprioritize it — obsolete items reduce transparency
Ask the Scrum Master to decide
Leave it for historical record
why: Transparency of the Product Backlog matters. Obsolete items clutter ordering and mislead inspection. Remove or deprioritize them explicitly.

q: 'Unrealized Value' in Evidence-Based Management refers to:
options: Sunk costs in the project
Potential future value if the product met all customer needs
Value lost to competitors
Value measured only in revenue
why: Unrealized Value (UV) is the opportunity — how much additional value could be created if the product better met customer needs. It informs investment decisions.

q: 'Ability to Innovate' in Evidence-Based Management measures:
options: Number of patents filed
Effectiveness at delivering new capabilities to meet customer needs
R&D budget size
Time in brainstorming
why: Ability to Innovate (A2I) captures how effectively an organization can deliver new capabilities. High technical debt, for example, degrades A2I.

q: A Product Owner must choose between a high-cost feature with uncertain payoff and a low-cost experiment to test the demand. The better empirical approach is:
options: Commit to the big feature to be decisive
Run the cheap experiment first to reduce uncertainty, then decide based on evidence
Split the Sprint evenly across both
Defer the decision to the Developers
why: Under uncertainty, small experiments that reduce the unknowns usually dominate large speculative commitments. This is empirical value management.

q: A Scrum Team is struggling to understand the business rationale behind product decisions. This suggests the Product Owner may not be adequately:
options: Working faster than the Developers
Communicating the Product Goal and ordering rationale
Attending all Daily Scrums
Writing the Definition of Done
why: One core PO accountability is explicitly communicating the Product Goal and the value driving ordering decisions. Without this transparency, the team cannot collaborate effectively on what matters most.

q: Who ultimately decides whether the organization respects the Product Owner's decisions?
options: The Product Owner
The Scrum Master
The entire organization
Stakeholders alone
why: The Scrum Guide 2020 states that for the PO to succeed, the entire organization must respect their decisions. Without this organizational backing, even good decisions are undermined.

q: A Product Owner can ask a Scrum Master to help them order the Product Backlog in exchange for assistance, but the PO remains accountable for the final order.
options: True
False
why: True. The PO may delegate tasks to anyone, including the Scrum Master. However, the accountability for ordering and the value decisions it represents stays with the PO.

q: A Product Owner does not have technical expertise in the domain the Developers work in. What is the impact on their ability to order the Product Backlog?
options: They cannot order it — they must ask the Developers to order instead
They can order based on value, market feedback, and strategic goals even without deep technical knowledge
They should avoid ordering and focus only on writing requirements
They need to hire a technical consultant to order for them
why: The PO's accountability is value and strategy, not technical implementation. They can order the backlog based on what maximizes value, with Developers providing technical trade-off input.

q: A executive stakeholder insists that a low-value item be added to the top of the Product Backlog. The Product Owner should:
options: Agree immediately to keep the executive happy
Refuse and remove the executive from future decisions
Acknowledge the input but order based on value, and explain the reasoning to the executive
Ask the Developers to override the decision
why: The PO is accountable for maximizing value. While stakeholder input is valuable, the ordering decision belongs to the PO. Transparency about the rationale builds trust even in disagreement.

q: Which characteristic is essential for a Product Owner to succeed in a distributed or remote team?
options: Ability to work independently without any developer contact
Consistent, transparent communication of goals and decisions across time zones and channels
Writing extremely detailed specifications to avoid questions
Delegating all communication to the Scrum Master
why: In distributed settings, the PO's explicit communication becomes even more critical. Without synchronous access, clarity and transparency in writing and asynchronous channels are essential.

q: The Product Owner is accountable for the Developers' velocity and their ability to forecast accurately.
options: True
False
why: False. The Developers are accountable for estimation and forecasting what they can accomplish. The PO is accountable for ordering and value, not for the Developers' capacity metrics.

q: A Product Owner discovers that stakeholders have been working directly with Developers to add items to the Sprint Backlog, bypassing the Product Backlog. The PO should:
options: Allow it if the items are valuable
Report this to the Scrum Master and restore the Product Backlog as the single source of work
Let the Developers decide what to do
Escalate to management without involving the Scrum Team
why: The Product Backlog is the single authoritative source of work. Bypassing the PO and the Product Backlog violates transparency and the PO's accountability for what gets built and in what order.

q: What is the relationship between the Product Owner's accountability and the Product Backlog's transparency?
options: They are unrelated
The PO is accountable for ensuring the Product Backlog is transparent so the team can inspect and adapt effectively
Transparency reduces the PO's accountability
Only Developers need to see the Product Backlog
why: Transparency is a pillar of empiricism. The PO's accountability includes making the backlog visible so the Scrum Team and stakeholders can inspect and collaborate.

q: A Product Owner spends most of their time in technical design meetings with Developers. What is a likely risk?
options: The team will move faster
Stakeholders and customer feedback are neglected; the PO is not focused enough on value and market feedback
The Scrum Master will complain
Nothing — this is a sign of strong collaboration
why: The PO's primary accountability is maximizing value by understanding stakeholders, market, and strategy. Spending excessive time in technical design may be a drift away from these core responsibilities.

q: When conflicts arise between different stakeholders, the Product Owner should primarily consider:
options: Which stakeholder has the most seniority
Which option maximizes product value and aligns with the Product Goal
Splitting the work evenly between both stakeholder requests
Deferring the decision to the Developers
why: The PO is accountable for maximizing value, not for keeping all stakeholders equally happy. Value and strategic alignment guide ordering, not seniority or politics.

q: A Product Owner is asked by their manager to ensure a specific technical tool is adopted by the Developers. The PO should:
options: Mandate the tool in the Product Backlog
Understand the business value the tool creates and order items accordingly; technical decisions belong to the Developers
Avoid the decision and let the Developers choose
Delegate the decision to the Scrum Master
why: The PO understands value and strategy. If a tool creates business value, the PO orders backlog items accordingly. But the technical decision to adopt it rests with the Developers, not the PO.

q: Which statement best reflects the Product Owner's relationship with the Definition of Done?
options: The PO writes the Definition of Done
The PO and the Scrum Team collaborate to ensure the Definition of Done supports value and transparency
The PO must follow the Definition of Done even if it slows releases
The PO changes the Definition of Done to meet release deadlines
why: While Developers and Scrum Master may lead Definition of Done discussions, the PO is part of the team and cares because it impacts the product's quality and long-term value.

q: A Product Backlog item has conflicting acceptance criteria from two different stakeholders. Who is accountable for resolving this?
options: The Scrum Master
The Developers
The Product Owner
The stakeholders must resolve it themselves
why: The PO is accountable for the Product Backlog, including clarity and coherence of items. When stakeholder input conflicts, the PO resolves it by making a value-based decision.

q: What does it mean for a Product Backlog item to be 'transparent'?
options: It uses fancy formatting
It is written clearly enough that the Scrum Team can understand what it asks for and why it matters
It includes every possible detail
It has been translated to multiple languages
why: Transparency means clarity about what the item does and why. Excessive detail is not transparency; shared understanding is.

q: Product Backlog refinement should happen only if there is a formal 'refinement event' scheduled.
options: True
False
why: False. Refinement is ongoing throughout the Sprint — in hallway conversations, reviews, pairing sessions, etc. While a time-boxed session can help, refinement is not a single formal event.

q: A Product Backlog item is vaguely described: 'Improve user experience.' The Developers push back during Sprint Planning. What should happen next?
options: Select the item anyway; Developers can figure it out
The Product Owner and Developers collaborate to refine it into smaller, clearer items before selecting it
Remove the item from the backlog permanently
The Scrum Master assigns the item to an individual Developer
why: This is the essence of refinement. When an item lacks clarity, the team works together to break it down and clarify it before selection. The PO leads, but refinement is collaborative.

q: How should the Product Owner decide whether to add a Product Backlog item suggested by a stakeholder?
options: Add everything stakeholders suggest
Reject everything that isn't from the Product Owner
Evaluate whether the item aligns with the Product Goal and delivers value relative to other items
Ask the Developers to vote
why: Anyone can suggest items, but the PO decides what enters the backlog. The decision is based on value and alignment with the Product Goal, not on who suggested it.

q: A Product Backlog item is estimated at 40 story points, far larger than most items. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Select it into a Sprint anyway
Delete the item because it is too large
The Product Owner and Developers should collaborate to split it into smaller items before selection
Increase the Sprint length to accommodate it
why: Large items reduce transparency and limit flexibility. Splitting makes the work clearer, testable, and deliverable more incrementally. This is a core refinement practice.

q: Two Product Backlog items deliver the same business outcome but with different risks. How should the Product Owner order them?
options: Randomly
By effort (smallest first)
Based on value, risk, and learning — choosing the approach that best achieves the Product Goal
Alphabetically
why: Ordering is not mechanical. The PO weighs multiple factors: value, risk, learning opportunity, and dependencies. The choice depends on what best moves toward the Product Goal.

q: What is the primary purpose of ordering the Product Backlog?
options: To organize work by complexity
To maximize value delivered by the Scrum Team over time
To reduce the number of items
To satisfy stakeholder preferences
why: Ordering guides the Scrum Team's work toward maximum value. It reflects the PO's best judgment about what to build next given current knowledge and strategy.

q: A Scrum Team discovers during refinement that two items in the backlog address the same user need. The Product Owner should:
options: Keep both to give stakeholders choices
Consolidate them into one clearer item and reorder as needed
Ask the Scrum Master to decide
Leave them as-is for the team to handle during Sprint Planning
why: Duplicate or overlapping items reduce clarity. The PO consolidates and reorders to keep the backlog lean, transparent, and focused.

q: Product Backlog items selected for the next Sprint should be more refined than items further down.
options: True
False
why: True. Items moving toward selection must be clear and ready to work on. Items far down the backlog can be coarser; refinement moves items from rough to ready.

q: During Product Backlog refinement, a Developer discovers that an item depends on infrastructure work that hasn't been started. The Product Owner should:
options: Select the item anyway and deal with the dependency later
Ask the Developers to do the infrastructure work first, without a backlog item
Work with the Scrum Team to surface the dependency, add it to the backlog if it's not already there, and reorder accordingly
Tell the Developer it's their problem to solve
why: Dependencies are ordering factors. If an item depends on unseen work, the PO works with the team to make dependencies explicit and handle them in the backlog.

q: What is a key indicator that Product Backlog refinement is happening effectively?
options: Every item has 100+ story points
Items near the top are clear, sized, and understood; Sprint Planning moves quickly
Refinement happens in a single formal meeting per Sprint
The Product Owner writes every item in advance
why: Effective refinement means the team can start Sprint Planning with confidence that top items are actionable. This shows ongoing collaboration and clarity.

q: A Product Owner wants to add an item to the Product Backlog that is purely technical debt cleanup with no direct customer value. The item should:
options: Be rejected because it has no customer value
Be added and ordered based on how it enables future value and supports the Product Goal
Be assigned to Developers to do in their 'free time'
Be hidden from stakeholders
why: Technical debt that blocks value delivery should be in the backlog and ordered. The PO recognizes that some items enable future value even if they don't directly deliver it today.

q: How does the Product Owner use the Product Backlog as a communication tool?
options: As a list of demands for the Developers
As a visible artifact that shows stakeholders and the team what will be worked on, in what order, and why
As a tool to assign work to individual Developers
As a compliance checklist
why: The Product Backlog is transparent and ordered by the PO. This transparency communicates priorities and strategy to everyone — team and stakeholders alike.

q: A Product Owner regularly reorders the backlog during the Sprint without discussing it with the Developers. What risk does this create?
options: The Developers will move faster
Developers may waste effort on items that are no longer priorities; trust and predictability erode
Nothing — the PO has authority to order
The Scrum Master will be upset
why: While the PO has authority, excessive mid-Sprint reordering without discussion can disrupt team focus. Transparency and collaboration on why ordering changes are necessary.

q: What is the relationship between Product Backlog refinement and the Sprint Goal?
options: They are unrelated
Refinement of items before Sprint Planning helps the team craft a meaningful Sprint Goal
The Sprint Goal determines all refinement
Refinement only happens after the Sprint Goal is set
why: Clear, refined items at the top of the backlog give the team better material from which to craft a Sprint Goal. Refinement and Sprint planning work together.

q: A Product Backlog item is: 'As a user, I want the dashboard to be faster.' This item is too vague. What is the most important refinement step?
options: Add acceptance criteria with specific performance targets (e.g., page load under 2s)
Assign it to the fastest Developer
Split it into 10 smaller items immediately
Have the Scrum Master estimate it
why: Clarity comes first. Acceptance criteria that define what 'faster' means let the Developers understand the ask and allow the PO to know when it's done.

q: The Product Goal is best described as:
options: A list of features to build in the current Sprint
A long-term commitment that guides the Scrum Team's direction and work over time
A detailed project plan
A technical architecture decision
why: The Product Goal is the overarching objective — the future state of the product the Scrum Team is working toward. It is long-term, not Sprint-scoped.

q: When should the Product Owner review and possibly revise the Product Goal?
options: Never — once set, the Product Goal must remain immutable for the team's commitment
Continuously, as evidence emerges about whether the Goal still serves customer and market reality
Only at fiscal year-end as part of the formal organizational planning cycle
Only when the Developers explicitly raise concerns about feasibility or value
why: The Product Goal guides long-term direction, but it is not sacred. When market evidence or learning shows the Goal is no longer valid, the PO inspects and adapts.

q: A Scrum Team has nearly achieved its current Product Goal. What should happen next?
options: Keep working toward it forever
Set a new Product Goal that reflects the team's learning and evolving market needs
Dissolve the Scrum Team
Hand off the product to another team
why: A Product Goal is achieved or becomes obsolete; it is not permanent. The PO and team define a new Goal that reflects progress and new priorities.

q: How explicit should the Product Goal be communicated?
options: Only to executives, not the team
Implicitly — developers should infer it from work
Very explicitly — in clear language that the entire team and stakeholders can understand
Only in technical documentation
why: The PO is accountable for explicitly communicating the Product Goal. Without clarity, the team cannot align their work or make good trade-off decisions.

q: The Product Goal should change every Sprint.
options: True
False
why: False. The Product Goal is long-term and stable. Sprint Goals change; the Product Goal typically remains consistent until it is achieved or becomes obsolete.

q: What is the relationship between the Product Goal and the definition of Done?
options: They are unrelated; commitments and goals operate on entirely different artifacts
The Product Goal commits the Product Backlog; the Definition of Done commits the quality of each Increment
The Definition of Done replaces the Product Goal once the Goal is achieved
The Product Goal lives inside the Definition of Done as a quality criterion
why: Both are commitments, but at different scopes. The Product Goal guides long-term direction; the DoD ensures each Increment is truly 'Done' and maintains quality.

q: If the Product Goal is to 'Build the best e-commerce platform in the market', what would make this Product Goal more effective?
options: Make the wording vaguer to give the team more interpretive freedom in execution
Specify measurable outcomes — e.g., 'achieve X% retention and Y% market share by Q4'
Remove the Product Goal and rely on Sprint Goals to direct the work each Sprint
Add more technical detail and specifications about the platform architecture
why: Clearer goals with measurable outcomes help the team and stakeholders know whether the Goal is being achieved. Vague goals obscure progress.

q: A Scrum Team is working toward a Product Goal, but halfway through a Sprint, market conditions change dramatically. The Product Owner should:
options: Keep the Product Goal unchanged to demonstrate commitment to long-term direction
Immediately cancel the Sprint and restart with a clean slate next Sprint
Inspect whether the Goal is still valid; if not, replace it with one reflecting the new reality
Delegate the Goal decision to the Developers, since they own day-to-day work
why: Empiricism applies to the Product Goal too. The PO inspects evidence and adapts. Clinging to an invalid Goal violates transparency and adaptation.

q: During a Sprint Review, a stakeholder says the completed Increment does not meet their expectations. What is the appropriate response?
options: Conclude the Developers failed to deliver and assign blame for the missed expectations
Inspect what was built, discuss the gap, and adapt the Product Backlog as the feedback warrants
Demand the Developers redo the work to match the stakeholder's original expectations exactly
Cancel the next Sprint until the team better understands stakeholder requirements
why: Sprint Review is a working session for inspection and adaptation. Feedback is a gift — it reveals misalignment between expectations and what was built, which informs the next steps.

q: Who should attend a Sprint Review, and what is their role?
options: Only the Developers; stakeholders are not invited
Only the Product Owner and Scrum Master; they report back
The Scrum Team and stakeholders; they collaborate on the Increment and next steps
The entire company in a formal presentation
why: Sprint Review is collaborative. The Scrum Team and invited stakeholders inspect the Increment together and discuss what to do next. It is a working session, not a presentation.

q: The Sprint Review is best described as:
options: A final acceptance meeting where stakeholders approve work before release
A working session where the Scrum Team and stakeholders inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog
An opportunity for the Scrum Master to report progress to management
A time-boxed meeting to collect stakeholder requests for the next Sprint
why: The Sprint Review is a collaborative working session, not a sign-off or approval meeting. The Scrum Team and stakeholders together inspect the Increment and update the Product Backlog based on new information.

q: The Scrum Team should invite stakeholders to the Daily Scrum.
options: True
False
why: False. The Daily Scrum is a Developers' event. The Product Owner and Scrum Master only attend if they are actively working as Developers on Sprint Backlog items. Stakeholders are not invited to the Daily Scrum.

q: During the first part of Sprint Planning, the Product Owner proposes:
options: A technical architecture for the Sprint
How the product could increase its value during the Sprint
Which Developers will work on which items
A schedule for Daily Scrum meetings
why: In Sprint Planning Topic One, the Product Owner proposes how the product could increase its value in the current Sprint. This becomes the foundation for crafting the Sprint Goal.

q: A sprint appears to be drifting away from its Sprint Goal mid-way through. The Product Owner should:
options: Cancel the Sprint immediately to start fresh with a more realistic Sprint Goal
Meet with the Developers to inspect whether the Sprint Goal is still achievable, and adapt scope
Add more items to the Sprint Backlog so the team can compensate for lost time
Stay silent and observe; the Developers will work it out by themselves eventually
why: The Product Owner is accountable for the Product Backlog and Product Goal. If the Sprint Goal is at risk, the PO collaborates with Developers to inspect the situation and adapt (by adjusting scope, clarifying Goal, or identifying blockers).

q: Which of the following are outcomes of the Sprint Retrospective? (Select all that apply)
options: The team identifies improvements to their process and tools
The team commits to experiments or actions for the next Sprint
Stakeholders provide feedback on the product
The Definition of Done may be updated
Product Backlog items are estimated
why: The Retrospective focuses on process and team improvements — how people interact, what tools help, and whether the Definition of Done is adequate. Stakeholder feedback and backlog estimation happen elsewhere.

q: The Product Owner has not attended several Sprint Reviews. Which of the following is most likely true?
options: The Scrum Team is functioning effectively without the Product Owner's input
The Product Owner is wisely avoiding direct conflict with difficult stakeholders
Stakeholders cannot properly inspect the Increment or adapt the Product Backlog without the PO
The Scrum Master should formally take over the Product Owner's accountabilities
why: The Product Owner's role in the Sprint Review is central — explaining the work, discussing the Increment, and facilitating Product Backlog adaptation. Without the PO, the Review loses critical context and decision-making authority.

q: During Sprint Planning, if the Developers believe they cannot achieve the proposed Sprint Goal with the selected items, they should:
options: Proceed and work overtime to meet the Goal
Discuss the concerns with the Product Owner and collaboratively adjust scope or Goal to ensure a realistic commitment
Cancel the Sprint
Work silently and report failure at the Sprint Review
why: Scrum values transparency and collaboration. The Developers must voice concerns about feasibility; the Scrum Team (including the PO) then adapts to craft a realistic, achievable Sprint Goal.

q: An Increment meets the Definition of Done on day two of a two-week Sprint. When should it be released?
options: Only at the end of the Sprint
Only after the Sprint Review
The Product Owner decides, weighing value, risk, and feedback timing — it could be released immediately
After all items in the Sprint are complete
why: An Increment can be released any time it meets the Definition of Done. Releasing earlier increases learning and delivers value sooner. The Product Owner makes this decision.

q: Multiple Increments can be created and released within a single Sprint.
options: True
False
why: True. An Increment is a stepping stone toward the Product Goal. Multiple Increments may be created within a Sprint, and each can be released independently once it meets the Definition of Done.

q: The Definition of Done is best understood as:
options: A checklist the Product Owner uses to accept completed work
A shared understanding of what it means for work to be 'complete' and potentially releasable
A list of testing procedures the QA team must perform
The final Sprint Review approval from stakeholders
why: The Definition of Done is a shared agreement among the Scrum Team about what 'complete' means. It applies to all work contributing to an Increment and is the commitment for the Increment artifact.

q: Work that does not meet the Definition of Done:
options: Should be counted as partially done and reported to stakeholders
Does not become part of the Increment and is not considered delivered
Can be released with a note explaining the gaps
Becomes part of the next Sprint's baseline
why: The Definition of Done is non-negotiable. Work below the DoD is not part of the Increment and cannot be considered delivered. It returns to the Product Backlog for later work.

q: A product has shipped three Increments so far. The Product Owner learns that customers are not using a major feature from Increment 2. The best response is:
options: Require the team to ship more features to offset the unused one and restore stakeholder confidence
Inspect why the feature isn't used and adapt — potentially stopping related investment elsewhere
Assume customers need more time to discover and adopt the feature; do nothing yet
Release a patch or notification that nudges customers toward using the feature
why: Empirical product management means learning from real-world outcomes. If a feature is unused, that is valuable evidence that should inform the PO's next ordering decisions.

q: A powerful executive pressures the Product Owner to add a low-value feature to the Sprint. The Product Owner should:
options: Comply immediately to maintain the executive's organizational support and goodwill
Refuse outright and escalate the executive's behavior to HR for review
Acknowledge input, explain value-based ordering, offer inclusion when priorities shift
Hide the request and hope the executive eventually forgets the demand
why: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing value. While stakeholders are important input sources, the PO makes the ordering decision independently, with clear rationale. Transparency about trade-offs builds trust.

q: The Product Owner should focus primarily on:
options: Keeping all stakeholders equally happy
Maximizing the value delivered by the product — balancing stakeholder needs with business strategy and outcomes
Delivering every feature proposed by the organization
Minimizing the team's effort
why: The PO's accountability is to maximize product value. This requires integrating many perspectives but making independent decisions about ordering based on value.

q: If a feature was already built and deployed, the cost of building it is sunk and should not influence whether to keep it in the product.
options: True
False
why: True. Sunk costs are irrelevant to future decisions. If a deployed feature delivers no value and consumes maintenance effort, the PO should deprecate it — past investment is not a reason to continue.

q: Evidence-Based Management Key Value Areas help organizations:
options: Eliminate all uncertainty from product planning
Measure and improve the value they deliver, using Current Value, Unrealized Value, Time to Market, and Ability to Innovate
Replace the Product Owner with automated dashboards
Predict future revenue with 100% accuracy
why: EBM provides frameworks (KVAs) for understanding and improving value delivery. It acknowledges uncertainty and uses empirical evidence — not prediction — to guide decisions.

q: Current Value (CV) in Evidence-Based Management refers to:
options: The original budget allocated to the product
The value the product is currently delivering to customers and the business
The projected value after all features are built
The developer salaries paid to build the product
why: Current Value measures what the product actually delivers today — customer satisfaction, revenue, usage, retention, etc. It is an outcome measure, not a financial or input measure.

q: A Product Owner wants to understand if a new feature will increase product value. The empirical approach is to:
options: Ask stakeholders to predict the impact
Build and release the feature completely, then measure customer response
Release a minimal version to real users, measure actual outcomes, and adapt based on evidence
Commission a consultant study
why: Empiricism means testing hypotheses with real users and real data. A minimal release (MVP-style) reduces risk and provides fast feedback for the PO's next decisions.

q: Ability to Innovate (A2I) degrades when:
options: The team's release frequency exceeds what users have appetite to absorb regularly
Technical debt, rigid architecture, or unclear DoD makes experimentation and capability delivery hard
Developer compensation rises faster than the team's measured Sprint output
The Product Backlog has grown beyond a size that can be meaningfully refined
why: A2I is the capacity to deliver new capabilities. Technical debt, poor code quality, and unclear standards erode this capacity, slowing innovation. Paying developers well does not degrade A2I.

q: The Product Owner forecasts delivery of a major feature in four Sprints. What is the most honest way to frame this forecast?
options: Guarantee that the feature will absolutely ship by the end of Sprint 24 as committed
State it as a range (e.g., 'likely 4–6 Sprints') based on current velocity, updated as learning accrues
Promise the feature by a specific date so stakeholders can plan with full confidence
Decline to forecast at all because empirical work cannot be predicted in advance
why: Empirical forecasting is transparent. Forecasts are probability statements based on evidence, not promises. They are expected to change as facts evolve.

q: A Product Owner should measure product success primarily through team velocity and story points completed per Sprint.
options: True
False
why: False. Velocity is a throughput measure, not a value measure. Success should be measured through outcomes — customer satisfaction, revenue, usage, retention — not activity.

q: The Product Owner learns that a committed feature will likely miss the expected release date. They should:
options: Hide the delay until the last possible moment to preserve stakeholder confidence
Share the evidence with stakeholders, discuss trade-offs (delay, scope, cost), and adapt the plan
Force the team to work weekends and longer hours until the schedule is back on track
Cancel the feature entirely and remove it from the backlog without further discussion
why: Transparency is a pillar of empiricism. Early visibility of risks allows stakeholders and the PO to make informed decisions together.

q: Two Product Backlog items deliver the same total value. Which should the Product Owner prioritize higher?
options: The one that takes longer to build
The one that delivers value to customers faster, reducing time-to-market for that capability
The one that was suggested first
They should be ranked equally
why: Time to Market is a KVA in EBM. When value is equal, earlier delivery of value typically creates more total value (due to compounding benefits and reduced risk).

q: A Minimum Viable Product (MVP) is best understood as:
options: A buggy or incomplete version shipped to cut corners
The smallest release that provides enough value to test a hypothesis with real users
The feature set that satisfies all stakeholder requests
A prototype shown only in demos
why: An MVP is a learning tool, not a shortcut. It is the smallest release that enables validated learning from actual customers about what delivers value.

q: The Product Owner should update their forecast when:
options: Only at the end of the quarter
Never — forecasts should not change
Regularly, as new empirical data emerges (velocity trends, complexity insights, stakeholder feedback)
Only when executives demand it
why: Forecasts are empirical tools. They improve as the Scrum Team learns more about the product, the domain, and their own capacity. Stale forecasts are inaccurate.

q: How should the Product Owner handle conflicting stakeholder priorities?
options: Give each stakeholder an equal vote on the final backlog ordering decision
Let the loudest stakeholder win to minimize ongoing organizational friction
Order the backlog by product value, incorporating all perspectives but making the final decision
Escalate the conflict to executive leadership and let them decide priorities
why: The Product Owner is accountable for value maximization. While stakeholders are important input sources, the PO weighs all perspectives and makes an independent ordering decision.

q: Unrealized Value (UV) in EBM measures:
options: Work that was started by the team but never finished or shipped to users
The gap between value customers realize today and what they could realize if needs were fully met
Money lost to inefficient spending or under-utilized engineering investment
Features that were deployed to production but never adopted by any users
why: Unrealized Value is the opportunity gap. It informs the PO's strategy: where is the biggest value gap, and how can the product close it?

q: An Increment may be released to users before the Sprint ends, as soon as it meets the Definition of Done.
options: True
False
why: Multiple Increments may be created within a Sprint, and any Increment that meets the Definition of Done may be delivered to users before the Sprint ends. Release timing is the Product Owner's decision; the Sprint is not a release boundary.

q: Time to Market (T2M) in Evidence-Based Management is important because:
options: Shipping faster always generates more revenue
The ability to deliver new capabilities quickly increases learning speed and competitive advantage
Developers should work longer hours
Marketing timelines determine product delivery
why: T2M is the speed of delivering new capabilities. Shorter T2M enables faster learning and earlier value capture — key competitive and product management advantages.

q: When three Scrum Teams contribute to the same product:
options: Each team has its own Product Backlog and Product Owner
They share one Product Goal, one Product Backlog, and one Product Owner accountable for value
One team manages the Product Backlog while others build
The Scrum Master coordinates backlog ordering across teams
why: Even at scale, a single product has a single Product Goal, single Product Backlog, and single Product Owner. This preserves a single source of truth for value direction.

q: When scaling to multiple Scrum Teams on one product, the Definition of Done should be:
options: Different for each team to avoid slowing progress
Decided by each team independently
Consistent across all teams so their work integrates reliably into a quality Increment
Set by the Product Owner and enforced strictly without team input
why: A shared Definition of Done ensures that all work — from all teams — meets the same quality and readiness standards. Consistency is essential when multiple teams integrate into one product.

q: Scrum is founded on:
options: Prediction and long-term planning
A prescriptive set of practices that must never change
Empiricism — transparency, inspection, and adaptation — and lean thinking
Hierarchical decision-making
why: The Scrum Guide 2020 explicitly states that Scrum is founded on empiricism and lean thinking. Empiricism drives decisions through transparency and learning; lean thinking eliminates waste and focuses on essentials.

q: Scrum is a methodology that prescribes exactly how to build products.
options: True
False
why: False. Scrum is a lightweight framework. It provides roles, events, and artifacts but is not prescriptive about engineering practices or methodologies. Teams adapt Scrum to their context.

q: The purpose of Product Backlog refinement is to:
options: Lock down all requirements before development starts
Add detail, clarity, estimates, and order to items so the Scrum Team can efficiently plan and execute
Reduce the Product Backlog size
Give the Product Owner full control over all technical decisions
why: Refinement is an ongoing collaborative activity that prepares the Backlog for effective Sprint Planning. It increases transparency and shared understanding without locking requirements.

q: A Product Owner realizes a decision was wrong based on actual customer feedback. They should:
options: Defend the decision to save face
Ignore the feedback and continue the original plan
Inspect the evidence, adapt the Product Backlog and strategy based on learning, and communicate the change transparently
Blame the Developers for not building it correctly
why: Empiricism means learning from reality and adapting. If evidence shows a decision was wrong, the PO pivots — this is not failure, it is intelligence.

q: The Product Owner should engage stakeholders:
options: Only at the Sprint Review
Only at the start of a release
Continuously throughout the Sprint whenever it helps clarify value, refine backlog items, or gather feedback
Through written status reports only
why: The Sprint Review is one structured engagement point, but the PO should collaborate with stakeholders continuously. Frequent interaction protects against building the wrong thing.

q: The Product Owner is pressured to lower quality standards to meet a deadline. They should:
options: Agree to release work below the Definition of Done to meet the committed deadline
Refuse the deadline pressure and cancel the Sprint to protect quality standards
Collaborate with the Developers on the trade-off and negotiate scope reduction to keep quality
Delegate the trade-off decision to the Scrum Master to mediate the conflict
why: The PO is accountable for long-term value. Shipping below the Definition of Done creates technical debt and erodes value. The solution is scope negotiation, not quality compromise.

q: A Product Owner should understand the organization's business strategy and ensure the Product Goal aligns with it.
options: True
False
why: True. The Product Goal translates business strategy into product direction. The PO bridges stakeholder/business needs and the Scrum Team's work.

q: When determining whether an Increment is ready for release, the Product Owner should check:
options: Whether all Sprints since the last release are complete
Whether the Increment meets the Definition of Done and whether releasing it creates more value than waiting
Whether the executive team approves
Whether the team has worked enough Sprints
why: The Increment is release-ready when it meets the Definition of Done. The release decision itself depends on value, risk, and market timing — the PO's judgment call.

q: The best Product Owner attitude toward changing requirements or pivoting the Product Goal is:
options: Resist change to protect the original plan
Accept all suggested changes immediately
Welcome evidence-based change while maintaining focus; adapt when evidence shows a better path
Avoid making any decisions
why: Empirical product management requires adaptability grounded in evidence. The PO maintains focus on the Product Goal but pivots when evidence shows a better direction.
